coredata.dc:title: Self-supported α-Ni(OH)2 nanosheet arrays modified with carbon quantum dots for high-performance supercapacitors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Wenjuan
Lu, Qingshan
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, self-supported and binder-free α-Ni(OH)2 nanosheet arrays decorated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs/α-Ni(OH)2) are prepared using one-step hydrothermal method. The optimal electrode exhibits high specific capacitance of 1724.0 F/g at 3 A/g, which is 2.3 times higher than the 750.7 F/g of bare α-Ni(OH)2. 45.8% of capacitance retention rate is achieved with the current density increasing from 3 to 50 A/g. Hierarchical structure of α-Ni(OH)2 and the assistance of CQDs endow CQDs/α-Ni(OH)2 with enhanced reactive sites and improved electrical conductivity, achieving high electrochemical performance. Asymmetric supercapacitors assembled with CQDs/α-Ni(OH)2 and activated carbon (AC) deliver a high-energy density of 44.0 Wh/kg at 3000 W/kg and retain an energy density of 29.03 Wh/kg at 18,015 W/kg. 99.91% of the initial capacitance and 99.46% of the Coulombic efficiency are maintained after 5000 cycles at 10 A/g. This study provides a new approach to design high-performance electrodes for supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thin films
Carbon
Quantum dots
Nanostructured materials
Electrochemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of NiOHC-0.2 and NF (a), the Raman spectrum of NiOHC-0.2, NiOHC-0, and CQDs (b), and XPS of NiOHC-0.2 (c-f).
SEM images of NiOHC-0 (a-b), NiOHC-0.2 (c) with different resolutions, TEM images of NiOHC-0.2 with different magnifications (d-f) (The insert in Fig. 2e is the particle-size distribution of CQDs).

CV curves of NiOHC-0.2 at different scan rates (a), CV curves of NiOHC-0, NiOHC-0.1, NiOHC-0.2, and NiOHC-0.3 at 20 mV/s (b), GCD curves of NiOHC-0.2 at different current densities (c), Dependence of specific capacitance on current density (d), EIS curves and equivalent circuit diagram (e), Coulombic efficiency and cyclic stability curves of the NiOHC-0.2 at 20 A/g for 5000 cycles (f).

CV curves of AC and NiOHC-0.2 at 10 mV/s (a), CV curves of NiOHC-0.2//AC at various scan rates (b), CV curves of NiOHC-0.2//AC under different potential ranges (c), GCD curves of NiOHC-0.2//AC at various current densities (d), Ragone plots of the NiOHC-0.2//AC (e), and Coulombic efficiency and cyclic stability curves of the NiOHC-0.2//AC at 10 A/g for 5000 cycles (f).

head.ce:title: Self-supported α-Ni(OH)2 nanosheet arrays modified with carbon quantum dots for high-performance supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology and Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Nanoscience & Nanotechnology, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot, 010021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology and Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Nanoscience & Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physical Science and Technology and Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Nanoscience & Nanotechnology, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot, 010021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, self-supported and binder-free α-Ni(OH)2 nanosheet arrays decorated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs/α-Ni(OH)2) are prepared using one-step hydrothermal method. The optimal electrode exhibits high specific capacitance of 1724.0 F/g at 3 A/g, which is 2.3 times higher than the 750.7 F/g of bare α-Ni(OH)2. 45.8% of capacitance retention rate is achieved with the current density increasing from 3 to 50 A/g. Hierarchical structure of α-Ni(OH)2 and the assistance of CQDs endow CQDs/α-Ni(OH)2 with enhanced reactive sites and improved electrical conductivity, achieving high electrochemical performance. Asymmetric supercapacitors assembled with CQDs/α-Ni(OH)2 and activated carbon (AC) deliver a high-energy density of 44.0 Wh/kg at 3000 W/kg and retain an energy density of 29.03 Wh/kg at 18,015 W/kg. 99.91% of the initial capacitance and 99.46% of the Coulombic efficiency are maintained after 5000 cycles at 10 A/g. This study provides a new approach to design high-performance electrodes for supercapacitors.

ce:sections.ce:para: Supercapacitors (SCs) are considered to be the promising energy storage devices owing to their high-power density and long cycle life [1–6]. SCs are divided into electrical double-layer capacitors and pseudocapacitors, which stores electrical energy via charge adsorption and Faradaic reactions, respectively [1,7–9]. Pseudocapacitors deliver higher specific capacitance compared to electrical double-layer capacitors. Among various pseudocapacitor materials, transition metal hydroxides are of much interest owing to their rich valence states and high theoretical capacitance. Particularly, layered α-Ni(OH)2 possesses large layer spacings in favor of ion diffusion, which delivers a theoretical specific capacitance of 2365 F/g [4,5,9,10]. In recent years, various nanostructured α-Ni(OH)2 such as nanoneedles [11], nanosheets [1] and nanoflowers [2] have been used for SCs. However, most of the α-Ni(OH)2 nanostructures are powder form, and the additional binders are needed in the fabrication of electrodes. This not only involves a complicated and time-consuming process, but also reduces reactive sites as well as increases the contact resistance [1,12,13]. However, the bare α-Ni(OH)2 still encounters poor conductivity [14,15], which limits the capacitive performance and the rate capability.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are the promising conductive dopants for electrode materials [16]. CQDs with low cost and high chemical stability exhibit excellent electrical conductivity [4,17,18]. In addition, CQDs possess large specific surface areas with rich oxygen-containing functional groups, which results in good hydrophilicity [17,19]. Recently, CQDs have been used to modify various electrodes in order to improve the electrochemical performance [16,20]. For instance, Bao et al. reported CQDs-doped α-Co/Ni(OH)2 hollow nanocages with a specific capacitance of 700 C/g at 1 A/g, and 79.93% of the initial capacitance was maintained at 10 A/g after 10,000 cycles [21]. Therefore, the rational decoration of CQDs towards binder-free Ni(OH)2 arrays probably produces remarkable electrochemical performances for SCs.

Herein, we propose a simple hydrothermal method to fabricate CQDs-modified α-Ni(OH)2 nanosheet arrays on nickel foam. Self-supported α-Ni(OH)2 exhibits hierarchical structure decorated with CQDs. The specific capacitance of 1724.0 F/g at 3 A/g and the capacitance retention of 97.54% after 5000 cycles at 20 A/g are achieved. Asymmetric supercapacitors assembled with CQDs/α-Ni(OH)2 and activated carbon (AC) exhibit an energy density of 44.0 Wh/kg at 3000 W/kg and retain an energy density of 29.03 Wh/kg at 18,015 W/kg.

The preparation and characterization of CQDs and CQDs/α-Ni(OH)2 as well as the fabrication of supercapacitor are illustrated in Supporting Information (Fig. S1). Fig. 1 (a) shows the XRD patterns of samples. The diffraction peaks are assigned to the (003), (101), (012), (015), and (113) crystal planes of α-Ni(OH)2 (JCPDS No.38–0715) [5,6,22]. Other peaks at 44.5°, 51.8°, and 76.4° are attributed to NF [9]. This indicates that crystalline α-Ni(OH)2 are grown on NF. No observation of CQD peaks is due to its low content and poor crystallinity. Fig. 1(b) shows the Raman spectra of samples. CQDs exhibit two Raman peaks at 1350 and 1581.6 cm−1 which belongs to the D and G bands, respectively [9,23]. For the NiOHC-0.2, except for the Raman peaks of α-Ni(OH)2, the weak G band of CQDs indicates the presence of CQDs in α-Ni(OH)2 arrays. Fig. 1(c-f) represents the XPS of NiOHC-0.2. The signals of Ni, C, and O in Fig. 1(c) are consistent with the chemical composition of NiOHC. The peaks of Ni 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 in Fig. 1(d) locate at 874.6 and 856.9 eV, respectively [23,24]. The energy separation of two Ni 2p peaks is 17.7 eV, which is accordant with the spectrum of Ni(OH)2 [4–6,22]. The peaks at 873.7 and 856.7 eV correspond to the Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2 of Ni2+, respectively, accompanying with the satellite peak at 862.8 eV. The peaks at 875.6 and 858.4 eV belong to the Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2 of Ni3+, respectively, as well as the satellite peak at 881.1 eV. For the C 1 s in Fig. 1(e), the peaks at 288.7, 286.0, and 284.7 eV reveal a main peak assigned to the CO, CO, and CC/CC, respectively [4]. Fig. 1(f) represents the XPS spectrum of O 1 s, and two peaks at 531.4 and 532.4 eV may be related to the NiOH bonds and surface-O-based species, respectively [23].

Fig. 2 (a-c) represents the SEM images of NiOHC-0 and NiOHC-0.2 at different resolutions, where the insets in Fig. 2(b, c) are the magnified images. α-Ni(OH)2 arrays on NF exhibit the hierarchical structure assembled with nanosheets. CQDs/α-Ni(OH)2 displays the morphology akin to bare Ni(OH)2. It is noted that the surface of NiOHC-0.2 is rougher than that of NiOHC-0. This suggests that the addition of CQDs can increase the specific surface areas, resulting in exposed active sites. Fig. 2(d-f) shows the TEM images of NiOHC-0.2 at different resolutions. The TEM images of CQDs are shown in Fig. S2. Hierarchical structure consisting of nanosheets is in accordance with the SEM results. This nanosheets-assembled hierarchical structure possesses large specific surface areas. As shown in Fig. 2(e), the CQDs marked with circles are uniformly distributed with the average particle size centering at 2.41 nm. The image of lattice fringes is shown in Fig. 2(f). The interplanar spacings of 0.197 and 0.232 nm match with the (018) and (015) lattice planes of α-Ni(OH)2, respectively [22]. This is consistent with the XRD results of α-Ni(OH)2. The interplanar spacing of 0.203 nm matches with the (101) lattice plane of CQDs [20], which gives the evidence of the introduction of CQDs into α-Ni(OH)2.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the CV curves of NiOHC-0.2 at various scan rates ranging from 1 to 50 mV/s. The CV curves of the other samples are given in Fig. S3(a-c). All the electrodes demonstrate a pair of redox peaks between 0 and 0.5 V [24], indicating that α-Ni(OH)2 stores electrical energy through a reversible redox reaction as follows: [3]
(1)
N i
(
O H
)
2
+ O
H
−
↔ N i O O H +
H 2
O +
e
−

As the scan rate increases, the oxidation and reduction peaks move to high and low potentials, respectively, originating from the polarization effect. Fig. 3(b) gives the CV area and peak intensity of each electrode at 20 mV/s. The CV area and peak intensity of α-Ni(OH)2 gradually increase with increasing the amount of CQDs. And they reach maximum values while the amount of CQDs equaling to 0.2 mg. This suggests that the introduction of CQDs is in favor of electrochemical reaction. The CV plots of NF and NiOHC-0.2 at 5 mV/s are shown in Fig. S3(d). The small CV area and weak peak of NF suggest that the influence of NF on the electrochemical properties of α-Ni(OH)2 is negligible. In other word, the charge storage is dominated by the redox reaction of α-Ni(OH)2. Fig. 3(c) shows the GCD curves of NiOHC-0.2 at different current densities. The GCD curves of the other samples are shown in Fig. S4. The nonlinear-shaped GCD curves indicate the Faradaic reactions of electrodes [7], which are in well agreement with the CV curves. The GCD curves of the NiOHC-0.2 are almost symmetric, indicating excellent reversibility and capacitive characteristic. The specific capacitance is determined according to the Eq. (1) shown in Supporting Information. Fig. 3(d) shows the correlation of specific capacitance versus current density for all samples. The specific capacitance of NiOHC-0.2 is the highest at each current density, which is about 1.4 times that of NiOHC-0.1, 1.5 times that of NiOHC-0.3, and more than 2 times that of NiOHC-0. Among them, the specific capacitance of NiOHC-0.2 is 1724.0 F/g at 3 A/g. 45.8% of the initial capacitance is maintained, while the current density increases nearly by 16.7 times. The high specific capacitance and excellent rate capability further corroborate that the addition of CQDs is favorable to the electrochemical properties. Fig. 3(e) shows the Nyquist plots with equivalent circuit. All curves exhibit the three parts including an intercept, a semicircle, and a straight line. The intercept of the real axis in the high frequency region represents the equivalent series resistance R es. The semicircular in the medium frequency region represents the charge transfer resistance R ct. Meanwhile, the straight line in the low frequency region reflects Warburg impendence Zw [4,20]. Using the equivalent circuit in the inset of Fig. 3(e), the fitted values of resistances are summarized in Table S1. The NiOHC-0.2 delivers an equivalent series resistance of 0.74 Ω and a charge transfer resistance of 1.29 Ω, which are lower than those of NiOHC-0, NiOHC-0.1, and NiOHC-0.3. This is probably attributed to that the hierarchical α-Ni(OH)2 arrays grown directly on NF keep close contact with NF. Meanwhile, the introduction of CQDs can enhance the conductivity, facilitating the electron transfer. In addition, the straight line of the NiOHC-0.2 is steeper than the others in the low frequency region. The Bode phase angle plots of the samples were investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, the phase angle of NiOHC-0.2 is closest to the ideal capacitive nature. The knee frequency of NiOHC-0.2 is higher than those of NiOHC-0, NiOHC-0.1, and NiOHC-0.3, suggesting the boosted conductivity related to low ion diffusion resistance [20]. The cycle life of the electrode is shown in Fig. 3(f). In the first 5 cycles, the charge/discharge curves have different shapes corresponding to the activation process of electrode. And the shape of the charge/discharge curves remains nearly the same as that in the last 5 cycles, which again confirms the stable electrochemical performance. After 5000 cycles at 20 A/g, the Coulombic efficiency maintains 98.91%, indicating that the reaction is highly reversible in consistence with the GCD results. The specific capacity is only lost by 2.46%, suggesting that NiOHC-0.2 has good cycling stability. This is owing to the hierarchical structure of α-Ni(OH)2 which sustains the volume variation during the charge/discharge process. This excellent cycling stability promotes the practical application of α-Ni(OH)2 as a SCs electrode materials.

Fig. 4 shows the electrochemical performance of the NiOHC-0.2//AC supercapacitor [4]. The GCD curves of AC are shown in Fig. S6(a). The mass ratio of positive and negative electrodes for NiOHC-0.2//AC is determined based on the charge balance in Supporting Information. Fig. 4(a) shows the CV curves of NiOHC-0.2 and AC at 10 mV/s. The potential windows of NiOHC-0.2 and AC are 0–0.5 V and −1–0 V, respectively. Therefore, the potential window of NiOHC-0.2//AC is in the range of 0–1.5 V. Fig. 4(b) shows the CV curves of NiOHC-0.2//AC at different scan rates. Quasi-rectangular shape indicates that the capacitance is derived from the combined contribution of Faradaic and electrical double-layer capacitive behaviors [8]. No obvious distortion of CV curve at 50 mV/s suggests the fast electron transfer rate and the rapid response of current to voltage. Fig. 4(c) shows the CV curves of NiOHC-0.2//AC under different voltage windows at 20 mV/s. The SC shows a rectangular-like CV curve from 0 to 1.6 V. The shape of the CV curve does not obviously change while the voltage above 1.5 V, indicating that a working voltage of 1.5 V is feasible. Fig. 4(d) shows the GCD curves of NiOHC-0.2//AC at different current densities, and the symmetric charge/discharge plots confirm excellent electrochemical reversibility. The relationship between current density and specific capacitance of NiOHC-0.2//AC is shown in Fig. S6(b). And the related equations for determining energy density and power density are shown in Supporting Information. Fig. 4(e) shows the Ragone plot of NiOHC-0.2//AC. The energy density is 44.0 Wh/kg, when the power density is 3000 W/kg. At the power density of 18,015 W/kg, an energy density of 29.03 Wh/kg still maintains. NiOHC-0.2//AC exhibits higher energy density and power density compared to the values reported in the literatures [2,7,12,25–29]. Fig. 4(f) shows the cycling ability of NiOHC-0.2//AC. 99.91% of capacitance retention rate and 99.46% of Coulombic efficiency are maintained at 10 A/g after 5000 cycles. The excellent performance of NiOHC-0.2//AC is owing to the binder-free and self-supported structure, which not only provides abundant reactive sites, but also reduces the contact resistance. In addition, the assistance of CQDs can enhance the conductivity, which facilitates the electron transfer in the Faradaic reaction, achieving improved electrochemical performance.

In summary, self-supported α-Ni(OH)2 nanosheet arrays modified with CQDs were prepared on NF using a one-step hydrothermal method. Hierarchical structure produces large specific surface areas with abundant available reactive sites. The additive of CQDs with uniform particle size improves the electrical conductivity of α-Ni(OH)2. The specific capacitance of NiOHC-0.2 reaches 1724.0 F/g at 3 A/g. The assembled NiOHC-0.2//AC supercapacitor exhibits an excellent energy density of 44.0 Wh/kg at a power density of 3000 W/kg. When the power density is 18,015 W/kg, the energy density of 29.03 Wh/kg is maintained. The excellent electrochemical performances are attributed to the rich reactive sites, layered structure, and high conductivity. This study provides an avenue to explore promising SCs as energy storage devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51962026).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.scriptamat.2022.115119.
